summary: Gruber presents a detailed technical conjecture about the iPhone D22 (later iPhone X) display specifications, arguing it will be a true @3x retina display at 462 PPI with no scaling required. Building on leaked iOS 11 code from the HomePod firmware that confirmed support for a 2436 × 1125 display, he challenges analyst Ming-Chi Kuo's prediction of a 2800 × 1242 display at 521 PPI. Gruber methodically demonstrates why 521 PPI makes no sense for an @3x display, since it would result in unnaturally small points per inch and require upscaling — a worse compromise than the Plus phones' downscaling. He suggests Kuo conflated specifications from two different iPhones, and that the 2800 × 1242 resolution belongs to a larger Plus-sized variant possibly coming in 2018.
key_points: The HomePod firmware leak confirmed iOS 11 supports a 2436 × 1125 iPhone display, with no mention of the 2800 × 1242 resolution Kuo predicted
A 521 PPI display doesn't make sense because dividing by 3 yields 174 points per inch, making all on-screen elements smaller than any existing iPhone — violating Apple's design consistency
The only natural PPI densities for @3x iPhone displays are 462 (154 × 3, matching Plus sizing) or 489 (163 × 3, matching non-Plus sizing), and 462 fits perfectly with 2436 × 1125 at 5.8 inches
Unlike the Plus phones which scale down from a virtual resolution (a reasonable compromise), scaling up on a 521 PPI display would cost more and look worse — the opposite of Apple's design philosophy
Leaked D22 schematics match the 2.17:1 aspect ratio of 2436 × 1125 exactly, while 2800 × 1242 (2.25:1) doesn't fit at all
money_quotes: A 521 PPI display doesn't actually make sense though.
It matters how big a point is, because it directly affects the real-world size of on-screen elements.
Scaling down is a reasonable and interesting compromise. Scaling up would be stupid.

Why would Apple pay more for a display and use scaling when they could pay less for a 462 PPI display on which they don't have to do any scaling at all? It would cost less, look better, and be more efficient.
The only reason to think otherwise is that Ming-Chi Kuo reported otherwise back in February. The simplest explanation is that Kuo got this wrong.

My understanding is that Apple does not even send prerelease builds of iOS to China. Instead, Apple employees fly prototype iPhones from China back to the US for testing with development builds of iOS.
key_insight: The leaked firmware, display physics, and Apple's design principles all converge on the same conclusion: the iPhone D22 is a true @3x display at 462 PPI with no scaling compromise, and Kuo's 521 PPI prediction never made mathematical sense for an iPhone.
